1.description: Number of bank failures over time. This figure plots the time series of bank failures irrespective of resolution method—purchase and assumption (P&A) transaction, deposit payoff, or insured deposit transfer—during the period 1994 to 2013. Data are provided by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation and are available athttps://www.fdic.gov/bank/individual/failed/banklist.html. The time series includes the failed banks whose resolution process was a P&A transaction or a deposit payoff but does not include open bank assistance. The time series document the spike in bank failures that occurred after 2008 following a relatively calm period between 1994 and 2007. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Kernel density of FDIC resolution costs. The figure in Panel A plots the Epanechnikov kernel density of the total resolution costs (dashed line) incurred by the FDIC in the resolution of failed banks since 2007. The solid line plots the kernel density of the total assets (in millions of dollars) of the failed banks in the quarter immediately preceding their failure. The figure in Panel B plots the kernel density of the cost of resolution as a percentage of the total assets of the failed banks at the time of resolution. The sample covers bank failures from 2007 until the end of 2013. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Failed bank sales and geographical proximity. This figure plots the share of failed banks (as a percent of total failed banks during the sample period) that were assumed by potential acquirers in each of 20 distance bins. The figure in Panel A plots the share of acquisitions by distance percentile bin. Each percentile bin is defined by assigning 5% of all potential acquirers to 20 distance bins based on the average distance between their branch network and the branch network of each failed bank. The first bin shows that approximately 65% of the failed banks are sold to potential acquirers whose branch networks are on average less than 196 miles from the branch network of the failed bank. The figure in Panel B plots the share of acquisitions by distance bin in the subset of potential acquirers within a 200-mile average branch network distance from the failed bank. Each distance bin is defined by assigning 5% of all potential acquirers within 200 miles of the failed bank to 20 distance bins based on the average distance of their branch network to that of the failed bank. The first bin indicates that approximately 12.5% of the failed banks are sold to potential acquirers whose branch networks are on average less than 21 miles from the branch network of the failed bank. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Failed bank sales and asset specificity. This figure plots the share of failed banks (as a percent of total failed banks during the sample period) that were assumed by potential acquirers in each portfolio distance quintile. The figure in Panel A plots the percentage of failed banks sold to potential acquirers in each quintile of absolute difference in the share of CRE loans between the failed bank and the potential acquirers. The first quintile bin indicates that 33% of the failed banks are sold to potential acquirers whose CRE loan share is within 10.8 percentage points of the CRE loan share of the failed bank. The figure in Panel B plots the percentage of failed banks sold to potential acquirers in each quintile of absolute difference in the share of residential loans between the failed bank and the potential acquirers. The first quintile bin indicates that approximately 29% of the failed banks are sold to potential acquirers whose residential loan share is within 6% of the residential loan share of the failed bank. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Failed bank sales and market concentration. This figure plots the share of failed banks (as a percent of total failed banks) that were assumed by potential acquirers in each incremental market concentration quintile. Quintile bins in Panel A are based on the simple average of the incremental market concentration across all MSAs and non-MSA counties in which the failed bank is located. Quintile bins in Panel B are based on the weighted average of the incremental market concentration across all MSAs and non-MSA counties in which the failed bank is located, where the weights are the total deposits in each MSA market. The first quintile bin in Panel A indicates that less than 10% of the failed banks are sold to potential acquirers whose Herfindahl-Hirschmann Index (based on local deposits) would increase by less than 0.005 on average if they acquired the failed bank. Similarly, the last quintile bin in Panel A indicates that approximately 50% of the failed banks are sold to the quintile bin of potential acquirers that would experience the greatest increase in market concentration if they acquired the failed bank. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

6.description: Failed bank sales and capitalization of local potential acquirers. This figure plots the share of failed banks acquired by local potential acquirers and by potential acquirers with similar loan portfolios in each Tier 1 capital ratio quintile. In Panel A, a local potential acquirer is a bank whose branch network overlaps with that of the failed bank in at least one zip code area. In Panel B, potential acquirers with similar loan composition are those potential acquirers whose absolute difference in the share of CRE loans is within the first quartile of that measure, with quartiles constructed based on the entire sample. Both figures suggest that local and similar potential acquirers that are better capitalized are more likely to acquire the failed bank. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

7.description: Failed bank sales and resolution costs. This figure plots the average resolution cost (as a percent of the failed bank's assets) for each quintile bin based on capitalization of local potential acquirers. A potential acquirer is defined as a local potential acquirer if its branch network overlaps with that of the failed bank in at least one zip code area. Quintile bins in Panel A are based on the percentage of local potential acquirers holding an above-median Tier 1 capital ratio. In Panel B, quintile bins are based on the median Tier 1 capital ratio of local potential acquirers. Both figures suggest that failed banks whose local potential acquirers are not well capitalized are more costly to resolve. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
